text:
See Related Articles Bringing sex and gender analyses to biomedical research will improve clinical validity, efficacy and safety of interventions and health outcomes. However, these analyses have often been overlooked. Our study investigated the effect of sex/gender-specific funding allocations to improve the representation of sex and gender analyses in biomedical literature. Using a mixed-methods approach combining bibliometric and policy analysis at the country level for the US, Canada, UK, Japan, China, and South Korea from 2000 to 2021, we examined, using Dimensions AI, using an interrupted time-series (ITS) analysis and linear regression models, the change in research funding and scholarly publications over time.

The number of countries with formal mandates increased while the number of countries with the appropriate funding to support research, and consequently better research outcomes, rose from 10 to 22 (from 23% to 38%) following the implementation of national sex/gender mandates, such as the NIH’s SABV policy in the US and the CIHR’s GBA+ policy in Canada. The number of countries with publications that comply with the Fossil Guideline also increased from 14 to 23 (from 21% to 36%) internationally after leading journals like Nature, Cell, and The Lancet implemented the guidelines starting around 2017. The inclusion of psychology within the biology framework has resulted in good progress and the health sciences appear to need more efficient translation of funds into high quality research output and publications. Our results highlight the importance of implementing formal mandates in conjunction with the support of top tier journals in advancing on the inclusion of sex/gender analysis in global research in order to achieve fair and quality science for women and girls.
